stem: A 58-year-old woman presents to a tertiary emergency department with 2 hours of sudden dyspnoea and pre-syncope, 8 days after a right total knee replacement at a Central Coast LHD hospital. She is alert but ashen and cool. Heart rate 132 sinus, blood pressure 76/44 mmHg unresponsive to 1 L of compound sodium lactate, respiratory rate 32, SpO2 88% on 15 L non-rebreather. Bedside transthoracic echocardiogram shows a severely dilated and hypokinetic right ventricle with septal bowing. Computed tomography pulmonary angiogram (CTPA) confirms bilateral central pulmonary artery filling defects. High-sensitivity troponin 480 ng/L (reference <14). Her surgical wound is dry and well-healed. There is no history of intracranial pathology, recent gastrointestinal bleeding, or stroke.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132, BP 76/44 after 1 L crystalloid, SpO2 88% on 15 L NRB, RR 32, T 36.6.
data_table.Echo: Severe RV dilatation and hypokinesis, septal bowing, no LV dysfunction.
data_table.Imaging: CTPA: bilateral central PA filling defects.
data_table.Bloods: hs-troponin 480 ng/L (<14), lactate 4.2 mmol/L, INR 1.0, platelets 240.
data_table.Surgical: Day 8 post right TKR; wound dry and clean.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next intervention?

options.text: Intravenous alteplase 100 mg over 2 hours plus unfractionated heparin
options.rationale: Haemodynamically unstable (high-risk) pulmonary embolism with persistent shock despite fluid resuscitation meets criteria for systemic thrombolysis. Alteplase 100 mg over 2 hours is the Australian first-line regimen. Recent major orthopaedic surgery is a relative (not absolute) contraindication; life-threatening obstructive shock outweighs the bleeding risk when no absolute contraindication is present.

options.text: Therapeutic enoxaparin 1 mg/kg subcutaneously twice daily
options.rationale: Low molecular weight heparin alone is appropriate for haemodynamically stable PE. In high-risk PE with shock, anticoagulation alone is insufficient because clot lysis is too slow to reverse obstructive shock; reperfusion therapy is required (eTG).

options.text: Apixaban 10 mg orally twice daily for 7 days then 5 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Direct oral anticoagulants are appropriate for stable PE but have no role in shock. Oral absorption is unreliable in shock states, and DOACs do not lyse existing clot. Surgical embolectomy or thrombolysis is needed (eTG).

options.text: Inferior vena caval filter placement after heparin loading
options.rationale: IVC filters prevent additional emboli but do not treat existing obstructive shock. They are reserved for absolute contraindication to anticoagulation or recurrent PE on therapeutic anticoagulation (eTG).

options.text: Bolus 1 L compound sodium lactate then reassess before further intervention
options.rationale: Additional fluid loading in RV failure worsens septal bowing and reduces left ventricular filling, precipitating cardiac arrest. Total fluid resuscitation should not exceed 500 mL in suspected RV failure before vasopressor support and reperfusion (ESC/RACP).

explanation.summary: High-risk PE is defined by sustained hypotension, shock or cardiac arrest from obstructive RV failure. Systemic thrombolysis (alteplase 100 mg over 2 hours) is first-line in Australia when no absolute contraindication exists; relative contraindications (recent surgery) are outweighed by mortality risk.
key_points: High-risk PE = sustained SBP <90 mmHg, vasopressor need, or arrest
Alteplase 100 mg over 2 h is the AU first-line lytic regimen
RV failure is preload-sensitive: cap fluid at 500 mL, start noradrenaline early
Absolute contraindications: intracranial neoplasm/haemorrhage, recent stroke <3 months, active major bleeding
explanation.context: Obstructive shock from acute PE kills by progressive RV strain: the thin-walled RV cannot generate enough pressure against the obstructed pulmonary vasculature, leading to RV dilatation, septal bowing into the LV, reduced LV preload, and systemic hypotension that further reduces RV coronary perfusion. Thrombolysis works because rapid fibrinolysis reduces pulmonary vascular resistance within minutes, unloading the RV before this spiral becomes irreversible.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Venous thromboembolism
ESC 2019 Acute Pulmonary Embolism Guidelines

subtopic_detail: Submassive PE - risk stratification and escalation
stem: A 71-year-old man with metastatic prostate cancer is admitted with 36 hours of progressive dyspnoea. CTPA confirms a large saddle pulmonary embolism. He is alert, blood pressure 122/74 mmHg, heart rate 108 sinus, SpO2 93% on 4 L nasal prongs. Echocardiogram shows moderate right ventricular dilatation with hypokinesis of the free wall and preserved apical contractility, tricuspid annular plane systolic excursion (TAPSE) 14 mm. High-sensitivity troponin 92 ng/L (reference <14), N-terminal pro-brain natriuretic peptide (NT-proBNP) 1850 pg/mL. Lactate 1.8 mmol/L. The simplified Pulmonary Embolism Severity Index (sPESI) is 3. He is on no anticoagulant; last fall was 3 weeks ago without head strike.

data_table.Echo: Moderate RV dilatation, free-wall hypokinesis, TAPSE 14 mm, McConnell sign present.
data_table.Imaging: CTPA: saddle PE with bilateral lobar extension.
data_table.Bloods: hs-troponin 92 ng/L (<14), NT-proBNP 1850 pg/mL, lactate 1.8 mmol/L, INR 1.1.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Therapeutic enoxaparin with monitored bed for haemodynamic deterioration
options.rationale: ESC intermediate-high risk PE (RV dysfunction plus biomarker elevation without shock) is managed with therapeutic anticoagulation in a monitored setting, with rescue thrombolysis reserved for haemodynamic decompensation. Routine thrombolysis in this group does not improve mortality and significantly increases major bleeding (PEITHO trial).

options.text: Systemic alteplase 100 mg over 2 hours plus unfractionated heparin
options.rationale: Routine systemic thrombolysis in normotensive intermediate-risk PE doubles the rate of major bleeding (including intracranial haemorrhage) without a mortality benefit. Reserved for decompensation (PEITHO; ESC).

options.text: Apixaban 10 mg twice daily for 7 days then 5 mg twice daily without monitoring
options.rationale: DOACs are acceptable for low-risk PE but the intermediate-high tier warrants parenteral anticoagulation initially with monitored bed for early decompensation. Cancer-associated VTE additionally favours LMWH or apixaban with cancer-specific evidence (eTG).

options.text: Catheter-directed thrombolysis with reduced-dose alteplase
options.rationale: Catheter-directed lysis is an emerging option in select intermediate-high risk patients but is not first-line in Australian practice and is limited to centres with interventional radiology expertise. Therapeutic anticoagulation with monitoring is the standard initial step (ESC/RACP).

options.text: Inferior vena caval filter and start warfarin with bridging heparin
options.rationale: IVC filter is reserved for absolute anticoagulation contraindication. Warfarin bridging is obsolete for acute VTE because of inferior outcomes versus LMWH/DOAC during initial therapy (eTG).

explanation.summary: PE is stratified into four tiers: high-risk (shock - thrombolyse), intermediate-high (RV dysfunction + biomarker rise, normotensive - anticoagulate + monitor), intermediate-low (one of RV/biomarker - anticoagulate), low (neither - consider outpatient). Routine lysis of intermediate-high PE worsens outcomes through bleeding.
key_points: PEITHO: routine lysis in intermediate-high PE = same mortality, more bleeding
Rescue lysis triggers: sustained hypotension, rising lactate, worsening RV
McConnell sign (apical sparing) is specific but insensitive for acute PE
Cancer-associated VTE: LMWH or apixaban preferred over warfarin
explanation.context: TAPSE measures longitudinal RV function; <17 mm indicates RV dysfunction. McConnell sign is RV free-wall akinesis with preserved apex, thought to reflect tethering by the LV. Together with troponin elevation (myocardial strain) and NT-proBNP (stretch), these define the intermediate-high tier that warrants escalation readiness without immediate lysis.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Venous thromboembolism
PEITHO trial - Meyer et al., NEJM 2014
ESC 2019 Acute Pulmonary Embolism Guidelines

stem: A 24-year-old woman with known asthma (Step 3: budesonide-formoterol maintenance and reliever therapy) presents to a HNE LHD emergency department with progressive wheeze and dyspnoea for 8 hours. She is tachypnoeic and speaking in 2-word sentences. After 1 hour of back-to-back salbutamol 8 puffs via spacer every 20 minutes, ipratropium 8 puffs every 20 minutes, and oral prednisolone 50 mg, her status is reassessed.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132, BP 138/84, SpO2 91% on 8 L Hudson mask, RR 32, T 37.0, peak expiratory flow 35% predicted.
data_table.Examination: Diffuse polyphonic wheeze, accessory muscle use, no silent chest, mild diaphoresis.
data_table.Bloods: Venous blood gas: pH 7.41, pCO2 36 mmHg, HCO3 22, K 3.4 mmol/L.
data_table.Treatment so far: 3 salbutamol/ipratropium nebulised sets, prednisolone 50 mg oral.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g over 20 minutes
options.rationale: Severe acute asthma not responding adequately to inhaled bronchodilators and systemic steroid escalates to intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g (or 10 mmol) over 20 minutes per Australian National Asthma Council guidelines. Magnesium causes smooth muscle relaxation through calcium channel blockade and inhibits acetylcholine release at neuromuscular junctions.

options.text: Intravenous aminophylline loading 5 mg/kg over 20 minutes
options.rationale: Aminophylline has a narrow therapeutic index and modest added benefit over inhaled bronchodilators. Australian guidelines list it as third-line after magnesium has been tried, given the risk of tachyarrhythmia and seizure (Australian Asthma Handbook).

options.text: Intravenous hydrocortisone 200 mg in place of oral prednisolone
options.rationale: Oral prednisolone is equivalent to intravenous hydrocortisone in patients who can swallow and absorb. Switching routes does not accelerate the steroid effect (which takes 4 to 6 hours regardless) and is unnecessary here (eTG).

options.text: Intramuscular adrenaline 0.5 mg
options.rationale: Intramuscular adrenaline is reserved for anaphylaxis or for severe asthma when bronchodilator delivery is impossible (peri-arrest, silent chest). Diffuse wheeze with air movement indicates ongoing delivery is feasible (Australian Asthma Handbook).

options.text: Initiate non-invasive positive pressure ventilation at 10/5 cmH2O
options.rationale: NIV in acute asthma has weak and inconsistent evidence. It is not recommended as a routine escalation step before maximal pharmacological therapy; intubation is the rescue if respiratory failure ensues (Australian Asthma Handbook).

explanation.summary: The Australian asthma exacerbation ladder is: continuous nebulised salbutamol plus ipratropium, oral or intravenous steroid, then intravenous magnesium sulfate, then aminophylline. NIV has limited evidence; intubation is reserved for impending arrest.
key_points: Magnesium 2 g (10 mmol) IV over 20 minutes is the third-tier step
Silent chest, exhaustion, paradoxical movement, drowsiness = pre-arrest
Normal or rising pCO2 in a tachypnoeic asthmatic signals impending failure
Aminophylline is fourth-line in AU practice (narrow therapeutic index)
explanation.context: Acute asthma physiology: bronchospasm and mucus plugging create dynamic hyperinflation, raising intrathoracic pressure and impairing venous return. The combination of tachypnoea and air-trapping initially blows off CO2; a normalising pCO2 in a still-tachypnoeic patient reflects ventilatory fatigue and is a red flag. Magnesium acts through calcium-channel antagonism on bronchial smooth muscle, complementing beta-agonist signalling without adding to tachyarrhythmia risk in young patients.

sources.label: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)
Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory - Asthma

subtopic_detail: Adult step-up - SMART therapy and ICS-formoterol
stem: A 32-year-old woman with adult-onset asthma uses salbutamol 6 to 8 puffs daily over the past month, has woken twice in the past week with cough and wheeze, and has used 2 short courses of prednisolone in the past 12 months. She is currently on budesonide-formoterol 200/6 microgram one inhalation twice daily as a preventer with salbutamol as a reliever. Spirometry today: forced expiratory volume in 1 second (FEV1) 68% predicted, FEV1/forced vital capacity ratio 0.66, bronchodilator response +14%. Inhaler technique is verified satisfactory; adherence is self-reported at 5 of 7 days per week.

data_table.Symptom control: Daytime symptoms most days, 2 nocturnal episodes/week, salbutamol 6-8 puffs/day.
data_table.Current preventer: Budesonide-formoterol 200/6 mcg 1 puff bd + salbutamol reliever.
data_table.Exacerbations: 2 oral prednisolone courses in last 12 months.

lead_in: Which adjustment best optimises her asthma control?

options.text: Switch to budesonide-formoterol 200/6 mcg as both maintenance and reliever (SMART)
options.rationale: Australian Asthma Handbook recommends SMART (single maintenance and reliever therapy) using ICS-formoterol for adults on ICS-LABA with suboptimal control. SMART reduces severe exacerbations versus same-dose maintenance plus salbutamol by capturing early symptom escalation with each rescue dose delivering both bronchodilator and anti-inflammatory.

options.text: Add tiotropium 2.5 mcg 2 puffs daily without changing current preventer
options.rationale: Long-acting muscarinic antagonists are a step-5 add-on for severe asthma uncontrolled on medium- to high-dose ICS-LABA. The next step here is SMART before adding a third controller (Australian Asthma Handbook).

options.text: Add montelukast 10 mg nocte and continue current preventer plus reliever
options.rationale: Leukotriene receptor antagonists are weaker controllers than escalating ICS-LABA dose or switching to SMART. The FDA boxed warning regarding neuropsychiatric effects (depression, suicidality) further limits routine use; reserve for aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease or rhinitis-dominant asthma (Australian Asthma Handbook).

options.text: Commence daily oral prednisolone 5 mg as maintenance therapy
options.rationale: Maintenance oral corticosteroid is a last-resort step in severe refractory asthma after biologic therapy has been considered. Two short courses per year is not severe-refractory; SMART comes first (Australian Asthma Handbook).

options.text: Refer for omalizumab subcutaneous injection
options.rationale: Omalizumab is restricted (under Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme criteria) for severe allergic asthma uncontrolled on high-dose ICS-LABA plus add-on therapy with documented IgE-mediated mechanism. This patient is on low-dose ICS-LABA still uptitrating (Australian Asthma Handbook).

explanation.summary: Australian step-up for adult asthma uncontrolled on ICS-LABA: confirm adherence and technique, then convert to SMART (single maintenance and reliever with ICS-formoterol). LAMA, biologics and oral steroids are reserved for higher steps after SMART optimisation.
key_points: SMART eligible: only ICS-formoterol (budesonide or beclomethasone-formoterol)
Reliever inhaler with maintenance ICS-formoterol replaces salbutamol entirely
Maximum 12 inhalations/day (including maintenance) for budesonide-formoterol 200/6
LAMA add-on (Step 5) before biologic; biologic before chronic OCS
explanation.context: SMART works because every symptom-driven reliever dose simultaneously delivers anti-inflammatory ICS, catching incipient exacerbations at the point of bronchoconstriction. Formoterol is the only LABA with rapid onset suitable for reliever use; salmeterol's slow onset precludes SMART. The mechanistic rationale is that asthma exacerbations begin with airway inflammation hours to days before symptoms peak, and frequent low-dose ICS micro-pulses interrupt that cascade.

sources.label: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)
Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory - Asthma

subtopic_detail: Near-fatal asthma - silent chest and intubation
stem: A 19-year-old man brought by ambulance is in extremis from acute asthma. He has had multiple presentations including one previous ICU admission. On arrival he is drowsy, slumped, with minimal chest movement. The chest is silent on auscultation. SpO2 84% on 15 L non-rebreather, heart rate 138 sinus, blood pressure 96/58 mmHg, respiratory rate 8 and shallow. Arterial blood gas: pH 7.18, pCO2 78 mmHg, pO2 56 mmHg, HCO3 24, lactate 5.2 mmol/L. He has received 3 salbutamol-ipratropium nebulisers, intramuscular adrenaline 0.5 mg, intravenous hydrocortisone 200 mg, and intravenous magnesium 2 g.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138, BP 96/58, SpO2 84% on 15 L NRB, RR 8 shallow, GCS 11.
data_table.Examination: Silent chest, minimal chest wall movement, diaphoretic, peripherally shut down.
data_table.ABG: pH 7.18, pCO2 78 mmHg, pO2 56 mmHg, HCO3 24, lactate 5.2 mmol/L.
data_table.Treatment so far: 3x neb salbutamol/ipratropium, IM adrenaline 0.5 mg, IV hydrocortisone 200 mg, IV magnesium 2 g.

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Rapid sequence intubation with ketamine and rocuronium
options.rationale: Silent chest, exhaustion, falling respiratory rate, drowsiness and a pH below 7.2 with rising pCO2 define near-fatal asthma requiring immediate intubation. Ketamine is the preferred induction agent for its bronchodilator effect and haemodynamic stability; rocuronium provides predictable paralysis without histamine release.

options.text: Bilevel non-invasive positive pressure ventilation 14/6 cmH2O via full face mask
options.rationale: NIV in life-threatening asthma with drowsiness, vomiting risk and impending arrest is contraindicated. Time spent on NIV when intubation is needed risks cardiac arrest from hypoxia (Australian Asthma Handbook).

options.text: Intravenous adrenaline infusion at 5 microgram/min titrated upwards
options.rationale: IV adrenaline infusion is sometimes used in refractory asthma but does not address the immediate respiratory failure and aspiration risk in a drowsy patient. Definitive airway control is required first (Australian Asthma Handbook).

options.text: Repeat intravenous magnesium 2 g over 20 minutes
options.rationale: Repeat magnesium dosing has not been shown to provide additional benefit and risks hypermagnesaemia (depressed reflexes, hypotension). Definitive airway control is the immediate need (Australian Asthma Handbook).

options.text: Heliox (70:30 helium-oxygen) via tight-fitting mask
options.rationale: Heliox has weak evidence and provides only 30% oxygen, insufficient for a patient already hypoxic on 15 L of high-flow oxygen. The patient cannot tolerate further hypoxia (Australian Asthma Handbook).

explanation.summary: Near-fatal asthma signs (silent chest, exhaustion, falling RR, drowsiness, pH <7.2, rising pCO2) mandate immediate intubation. Ketamine-rocuronium RSI provides bronchodilation with predictable paralysis; ventilator strategy uses low tidal volumes, low respiratory rate and prolonged expiration to minimise dynamic hyperinflation.
key_points: Near-fatal markers: silent chest, GCS drop, exhaustion, pH <7.2, rising pCO2
Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV is preferred induction (bronchodilator)
Ventilator: TV 5-7 mL/kg, RR 8-10, I:E 1:4, permissive hypercapnia
Disconnect from ventilator if circulatory collapse (auto-PEEP tension)
explanation.context: Intubated asthmatics die from dynamic hyperinflation rather than hypoxia: bronchospasm prolongs expiration, breath stacking raises intrathoracic pressure, venous return falls and circulation collapses. The ventilator strategy intentionally undershoots minute ventilation to allow full expiration between breaths. Permissive hypercapnia (pCO2 to 80-90 mmHg, pH down to 7.15) is tolerated in exchange for avoiding breath-stacking arrest. If circulatory collapse occurs, disconnect the circuit to release trapped gas before reconnecting.

sources.label: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)
Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory - Asthma

stem: A 68-year-old man with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), 40-pack-year smoking history and previous FEV1 42% predicted presents with 4 days of worsening dyspnoea, increased sputum volume and a change from clear to yellow-green sputum. He is afebrile, heart rate 96, blood pressure 132/78, respiratory rate 22, SpO2 88% on room air, 92% on 2 L nasal prongs. Chest x-ray shows hyperinflation without consolidation. He has had 2 exacerbations in the past 12 months and lives independently with his wife.

data_table.Examination: Bilateral wheeze, prolonged expiration, no crackles, no consolidation.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate antibiotic decision?

options.text: Amoxicillin 500 mg orally 8-hourly for 5 days
options.rationale: He meets all three Anthonisen criteria (increased dyspnoea, sputum volume, sputum purulence), defining a type 1 exacerbation likely to benefit from antibiotics. Amoxicillin is first-line in Australia for COPD exacerbations targeting Haemophilus influenzae, Streptococcus pneumoniae and Moraxella catarrhalis.

options.text: Doxycycline 100 mg orally 12-hourly for 5 days
options.rationale: Doxycycline is the second-line oral option in non-severe penicillin hypersensitivity. Amoxicillin remains first-line when no penicillin allergy is documented (eTG).

options.text: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg orally 12-hourly for 7 days
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is reserved for severe exacerbations, treatment failure, or pseudomonal risk (frequent antibiotics, structural lung disease). The added clavulanate increases diarrhoea risk without first-line benefit here (eTG).

options.text: Azithromycin 500 mg orally daily for 3 days
options.rationale: Macrolides have unreliable activity against Haemophilus influenzae and Australian Streptococcus pneumoniae resistance is significant. Long-term azithromycin has a role in exacerbation prevention in selected patients but not as acute monotherapy (eTG).

options.text: Withhold antibiotic and treat with bronchodilators and steroid alone
options.rationale: Withholding antibiotics is reasonable when fewer than two Anthonisen criteria are met or sputum is not purulent. He meets all three, making antibiotics indicated (eTG).

explanation.summary: COPD exacerbations are stratified by the Anthonisen criteria: increased dyspnoea, sputum volume, sputum purulence. Antibiotics benefit type 1 (all three) and type 2 (two of three including purulence). Amoxicillin is first-line in Australia for non-severe exacerbations; amoxicillin-clavulanate is reserved for severe disease or pseudomonal risk.
key_points: Anthonisen type 1 (all 3) or type 2 with purulence = antibiotics indicated
Amoxicillin 500 mg 8-hourly 5 days is AU first-line
Pseudomonal risk factors: bronchiectasis, frequent antibiotics, prior Pseudomonas
Always pair with bronchodilator escalation and 5-day prednisolone 30-50 mg
explanation.context: COPD bacterial flora reflects the chronically colonised airway. Haemophilus influenzae is most common, followed by pneumococcus and Moraxella; Pseudomonas appears in advanced disease and bronchiectasis. Sputum purulence (green/yellow) correlates with bacterial bioburden and neutrophilic myeloperoxidase, providing a clinically useful proxy for when antimicrobial therapy adds value over bronchodilator/steroid alone.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory - COPD
Lung Foundation Australia COPD-X Concise Guide

subtopic_detail: Stable COPD - inhaler escalation by phenotype
stem: A 72-year-old man with COPD (post-bronchodilator FEV1 48% predicted) is reviewed in the respiratory clinic. He has been on tiotropium 18 mcg once daily for 18 months. He reports increasing dyspnoea on walking the dog and 3 moderate exacerbations in the past 12 months (one hospitalised). He has no asthma history. Eosinophil count is 0.42 x10^9/L (reference 0.02-0.5).

data_table.Symptoms: Modified MRC dyspnoea 3, COPD assessment test 22.
data_table.Lung function: Post-BD FEV1 48% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.58, no asthma reversibility.
data_table.Exacerbations: 3 moderate exacerbations in past 12 months, 1 hospitalisation.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Add an inhaled corticosteroid-long-acting beta agonist combination to current tiotropium
options.rationale: Frequent exacerbations (>=2 moderate or >=1 severe) plus eosinophil count >=0.3 x10^9/L predict ICS responsiveness. Escalating from LAMA monotherapy to triple therapy (LAMA + LABA + ICS) reduces exacerbations in this phenotype per COPD-X.

options.text: Replace tiotropium with inhaled corticosteroid monotherapy
options.rationale: ICS monotherapy is inferior to LABA or LAMA in COPD and is not recommended as monotherapy in Australian guidelines. The bronchodilator should be retained (COPD-X).

options.text: Add long-acting beta agonist to tiotropium without inhaled corticosteroid
options.rationale: LAMA + LABA dual therapy is appropriate when exacerbations are infrequent or eosinophil count is low (<0.1 x10^9/L). With high eosinophils and frequent exacerbations, ICS adds exacerbation-reduction benefit (COPD-X).

options.text: Add azithromycin 250 mg orally three times weekly
options.rationale: Prophylactic macrolide is a step-up option in exacerbation-prone COPD already on optimal triple therapy, not before escalating from LAMA monotherapy. Cardiac and QT screening is also required before initiating (COPD-X).

options.text: Add oral theophylline 200 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Theophylline is rarely used in modern Australian practice because of narrow therapeutic index, drug interactions and limited efficacy versus inhaled bronchodilators. It is not the next step from LAMA monotherapy (COPD-X).

explanation.summary: COPD pharmacotherapy is escalated by symptoms and exacerbation frequency. Eosinophil count predicts ICS responsiveness: >=0.3 x10^9/L favours ICS-containing regimens, <0.1 argues against. Triple therapy (LAMA + LABA + ICS) is the next step from LAMA monotherapy in the exacerbator phenotype with high eosinophils.
key_points: Exacerbator phenotype + eosinophils >=0.3 = ICS adds value
Low eosinophils (<0.1) and frequent exacerbations: LAMA + LABA instead
ICS in COPD increases pneumonia risk (counsel; do not avoid if indicated)
Azithromycin prophylaxis: step-up after triple therapy; check QTc and audiogram
explanation.context: COPD exacerbation prevention has shifted from a single ladder to phenotype-driven choice. The blood eosinophil count is a surrogate for type-2 airway inflammation that responds to corticosteroid suppression. Low-eosinophil exacerbators behave more like neutrophilic airways disease where ICS adds pneumonia risk without exacerbation benefit; LABA-LAMA dual bronchodilation suits this group. Triple therapy via single inhaler improves adherence over multi-device regimens.

sources.label: Lung Foundation Australia COPD-X Concise Guide
Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory - COPD

stem: A 74-year-old woman with severe COPD presents with acute exacerbation. Despite 90 minutes of optimised medical therapy (controlled oxygen via Venturi mask titrated to SpO2 88-92%, nebulised salbutamol and ipratropium, oral prednisolone 50 mg, intravenous ceftriaxone), she remains dyspnoeic. She is alert and cooperative. Arterial blood gas: pH 7.26, pCO2 78 mmHg, pO2 62 mmHg on 28% FiO2, HCO3 32 mmol/L. There is no pneumothorax on chest x-ray.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110, BP 142/86, SpO2 90% on 28% Venturi, RR 28, alert.
data_table.ABG: pH 7.26, pCO2 78 mmHg, pO2 62 mmHg on 28% FiO2, HCO3 32, BE +5.
data_table.Imaging: CXR: hyperinflation, no pneumothorax, no consolidation.
data_table.Medical therapy so far: 90 min: controlled O2, neb bronchodilator, oral prednisolone, IV antibiotic.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Bilevel non-invasive ventilation starting at IPAP 12 EPAP 5 cmH2O
options.rationale: Acute hypercapnic respiratory failure (pH <7.35, pCO2 >45) in COPD that persists despite 60 minutes of optimised medical therapy meets criteria for bilevel NIV. Starting pressures of IPAP 10-15 / EPAP 4-6 cmH2O are titrated upward to a tidal volume of 6-8 mL/kg, pH normalisation and respiratory rate reduction.
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand position statement on NIV

options.text: Increase oxygen via Hudson mask to 8 L/min and reassess in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Uncontrolled oxygen in hypercapnic COPD worsens ventilation-perfusion mismatch (Haldane effect), increases dead space ventilation and risks oxygen-induced hypercapnia. Target SpO2 in COPD is 88-92% with controlled delivery (TSANZ).
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand position statement on NIV

options.text: Intubate and commence invasive mechanical ventilation
options.rationale: Intubation is reserved for NIV contraindication (vomiting, agitation, decreased consciousness, facial trauma) or NIV failure. NIV is the first-line escalation in alert, cooperative COPD exacerbators with hypercapnic acidosis (TSANZ).
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand position statement on NIV

options.text: Intravenous doxapram infusion as a respiratory stimulant
options.rationale: Doxapram is largely obsolete because of neurological side effects (agitation, seizures) and inferior outcomes versus NIV. It has no routine role in modern Australian practice (TSANZ).
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand position statement on NIV

options.text: Continuous positive airway pressure at 7.5 cmH2O via full face mask
options.rationale: CPAP supports oxygenation and reduces work of breathing but does not augment tidal volume and therefore does not clear CO2 effectively in hypercapnic failure. Bilevel NIV (with inspiratory pressure support) is required (TSANZ).
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand position statement on NIV

explanation.summary: Type 2 respiratory failure (pCO2 >45 mmHg) in COPD that does not resolve with optimised medical therapy escalates to bilevel NIV. IPAP supports tidal volume (clears CO2); EPAP recruits alveoli (improves oxygenation). Type 1 failure (pO2 <60 mmHg, normal pCO2) responds primarily to oxygen and CPAP if recruitment is needed.
key_points: NIV in COPD: start IPAP 10-15 / EPAP 4-6, titrate to TV 6-8 mL/kg, pH, RR
Reassess at 1-2 h: pH improving + RR falling = success; if not = consider intubation
Contraindications: vomiting, decreased consciousness, agitation, facial trauma
CPAP suits type 1 failure (e.g. pulmonary oedema); bilevel suits type 2
explanation.context: The Haldane effect explains oxygen-induced hypercapnia: oxygenated haemoglobin releases CO2 (Bohr effect in reverse), and hyperoxia abolishes hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction, redistributing blood to poorly ventilated alveoli and increasing dead space. In severe COPD with chronic CO2 retention, this can raise pCO2 by 10-15 mmHg over an hour. Controlled oxygen to SpO2 88-92% preserves hypoxic drive enough to maintain ventilation while avoiding hypoxic injury.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory - COPD
Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand position statement on NIV

subtopic_detail: Type 1 vs type 2 failure - mechanism inversion
stem: A medical registrar is teaching about respiratory failure. She draws four pathophysiological mechanisms on the board: (1) ventilation-perfusion mismatch with preserved alveolar ventilation; (2) alveolar hypoventilation with normal alveolar-arterial oxygen gradient; (3) right-to-left shunt unresponsive to oxygen therapy; (4) impaired diffusion across the alveolar-capillary membrane.
lead_in: Which clinical scenario best matches pattern 2 (alveolar hypoventilation with normal A-a gradient)?

options.text: Opioid overdose with respiratory rate 6 and pinpoint pupils
options.rationale: Pure alveolar hypoventilation (depressed respiratory drive) produces hypoxaemia and hypercapnia with a preserved A-a gradient because gas exchange surfaces are normal. Opioid overdose suppresses brainstem respiratory drive without lung pathology. Reversal with naloxone normalises the picture.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness - Opioid

options.text: Acute pulmonary embolism with sudden dyspnoea and SpO2 88%
options.rationale: PE produces V/Q mismatch (pattern 1) with widened A-a gradient because perfusion to obstructed segments is reduced while ventilation continues. Hyperventilation initially lowers pCO2 (West).

options.text: Severe lobar pneumonia with consolidation and oxygen-refractory hypoxaemia
options.rationale: Consolidated lobe behaves as a shunt (pattern 3): blood perfuses non-ventilated alveoli, so oxygen supplementation cannot correct the shunted fraction. A-a gradient is markedly widened (West).

options.text: Pulmonary fibrosis with chronic exertional dyspnoea and clubbing
options.rationale: Fibrosis causes diffusion impairment (pattern 4) with widened A-a gradient. The thickened alveolar-capillary membrane reduces oxygen transfer, especially during exercise when transit time is shortened (West).

options.text: Acute severe asthma with diffuse wheeze and rising pCO2
options.rationale: Asthma combines V/Q mismatch (pattern 1) with later alveolar hypoventilation when fatigue supervenes, but the A-a gradient is widened because of mismatch from bronchoconstriction (West).

explanation.summary: Pure alveolar hypoventilation (low respiratory drive or neuromuscular failure) is the only mechanism producing hypercapnia with a normal A-a gradient because the lungs themselves are normal. Every other mechanism widens A-a gradient because gas exchange is disturbed at the alveolar level.
key_points: A-a gradient = PAO2 - PaO2; normal ~5-15 mmHg (rises with age and FiO2)
Normal A-a + hypercapnia = central (opioid, brainstem) or neuromuscular cause
Widened A-a = lung pathology: V/Q mismatch, shunt, diffusion
Shunt fraction does not improve with supplemental O2; V/Q does
explanation.context: The A-a gradient is the bedside tool that distinguishes a lung problem from a pump problem. When PAO2 (calculated from the alveolar gas equation) exceeds PaO2 (measured on ABG) by an amount above the age-predicted upper limit, oxygenation has failed at the alveolar interface and the cause is intrapulmonary. When PAO2 closely matches PaO2 despite hypoxaemia, the lung is innocent and the failure is upstream: brainstem drive (opioids, stroke), spinal cord (high cervical injury), neuromuscular junction (myasthenia), or muscle (Guillain-Barre weakness).

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness - Opioid overdose
West's Respiratory Physiology (cited in RACP curriculum)

stem: A 26-year-old man is hit in the chest while playing rugby. Brought to the emergency department 30 minutes later he is increasingly agitated, with severe dyspnoea, asymmetric chest expansion, hyper-resonance over the right hemithorax with absent breath sounds, and tracheal deviation to the left. Heart rate 138, blood pressure 78/44, SpO2 84% on 15 L non-rebreather, distended neck veins.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138, BP 78/44, SpO2 84% on 15 L NRB, RR 36, agitated.
data_table.Examination: Right hemithorax: hyper-resonant, absent breath sounds, reduced expansion; trachea deviated left; raised JVP.

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Needle thoracostomy at right second intercostal space mid-clavicular line
options.rationale: Traditional needle decompression site (2nd ICS MCL) has higher failure rates in adults because of chest wall thickness, particularly in obese or muscular patients. The 5th ICS anterior axillary line (lateral approach) is now preferred in adult trauma per ATLS 10th edition and ANZCOR.

options.text: Needle thoracostomy at right fifth intercostal space anterior axillary line
options.rationale: Updated ATLS/ANZCOR guidance recommends needle decompression at the 5th intercostal space anterior axillary line in adults because chest wall is thinner and the lateral approach is more reliable. This is followed by definitive tube thoracostomy at the same site.

options.text: Urgent erect chest x-ray to confirm pneumothorax before intervention
options.rationale: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis treated before imaging. Delay for x-ray risks cardiac arrest from progressive obstructed venous return (ANZCOR; ATLS).

options.text: Bedside thoracic ultrasound to confirm absent lung sliding
options.rationale: Thoracic ultrasound is fast and accurate for pneumothorax but should not delay decompression when classical clinical signs are present in a shocked trauma patient. Use it only if it can be obtained without delaying intervention (ATLS).

options.text: Insert 32 French intercostal catheter immediately as primary intervention
options.rationale: Tube thoracostomy is the definitive treatment but takes longer to set up than needle decompression. In a peri-arrest patient, immediate needle decompression buys time for tube insertion (ANZCOR/ATLS).

explanation.summary: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis (haemodynamic instability + ipsilateral hyperresonance + reduced breath sounds + tracheal deviation away). Immediate needle decompression at the 5th ICS anterior axillary line (updated site) precedes definitive intercostal catheter insertion.
key_points: Tension PTX = clinical diagnosis; do not delay for imaging
Updated needle site: 5th ICS anterior axillary line (was 2nd ICS MCL)
Definitive: intercostal catheter, 28-32 Fr, same lateral site
Mechanism: one-way valve trapping intrapleural gas, mediastinal shift, obstructed venous return
explanation.context: Tension pneumothorax kills by progressive accumulation of intrapleural gas that cannot escape. The ipsilateral lung collapses, the mediastinum shifts contralaterally, kinking the great vessels and obstructing venous return. Cardiac output falls until arrest. The needle creates a controlled communication that converts tension to simple pneumothorax, restoring venous return immediately. The 5th ICS anterior axillary line was chosen on the basis of CT chest-wall thickness studies showing many adults have >5 cm of soft tissue at the traditional 2nd ICS MCL site, exceeding standard angiocath length.

sources.label: ANZCOR Guideline 11.8 - Targeted Temperature and Trauma
ATLS 10th edition (Royal Australasian College of Surgeons)

stem: A 22-year-old tall, thin male non-smoker presents with sudden right-sided pleuritic chest pain and mild dyspnoea that began 6 hours ago while sitting still. He has no past medical history. He is comfortable, heart rate 88, blood pressure 124/76, SpO2 97% room air, respiratory rate 16. Chest x-ray shows a right-sided pneumothorax measuring 1.8 cm at the level of the hilum (interpleural distance). There is no mediastinal shift.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 88, BP 124/76, SpO2 97% RA, RR 16, comfortable.
data_table.Imaging: Right pneumothorax, interpleural distance 1.8 cm at hilar level, no mediastinal shift.
data_table.Examination: Mildly reduced right-sided breath sounds, no tracheal deviation.
data_table.History: First episode, primary (no underlying lung disease), non-smoker.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Needle aspiration via the 2nd intercostal space mid-clavicular line
options.rationale: Needle aspiration was previously preferred but the BTS/TSANZ-endorsed PSP trial (NEJM 2020) showed conservative management is non-inferior for small-to-moderate primary spontaneous pneumothorax with fewer complications. Aspiration remains an option but is no longer first-line for stable patients.

options.text: Conservative observation with high-flow oxygen and admission
options.rationale: Stable primary spontaneous pneumothorax of moderate size (1-2 cm) in a well patient is managed conservatively per TSANZ. High-flow oxygen accelerates pleural air resorption fourfold by establishing a nitrogen gradient. Admission allows monitoring and serial imaging.

options.text: Insertion of 14 French Seldinger intercostal catheter to underwater seal
options.rationale: Intercostal catheter is reserved for large (>2 cm interpleural distance with symptoms), tension, or failed conservative or aspiration management. Smaller-bore Seldinger catheters are preferred over surgical drains when needed (TSANZ).

options.text: Discharge home with primary care follow-up in 1 week
options.rationale: Stable small primary pneumothorax can be discharged with early follow-up, but moderate size (1-2 cm) typically warrants admission for observation and repeat x-ray at 4-6 hours to confirm stability (TSANZ).

options.text: Surgical referral for video-assisted thoracoscopic bullectomy and pleurodesis
options.rationale: Surgical intervention is reserved for first episode with prolonged air leak, recurrent ipsilateral pneumothorax, contralateral pneumothorax, or specific occupations (pilots, divers). First episode managed conservatively first (TSANZ).

explanation.summary: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax management has shifted toward conservative care for stable patients (PSP trial). Large or symptomatic pneumothoraces (>2 cm at hilum with dyspnoea) receive intervention; small or moderate stable pneumothoraces are observed with high-flow oxygen.
key_points: PSP trial: conservative management non-inferior to drainage, fewer complications
High-flow O2 accelerates pleural air resorption by ~4-fold
Size measured as interpleural distance at hilar level on erect CXR
VATS bullectomy + pleurodesis for recurrence or persistent air leak >5 days
explanation.context: The conservative shift was driven by recognition that interventions cause iatrogenic complications (pain, infection, prolonged hospitalisation) without changing the natural history of pneumothorax resorption. High-flow oxygen works through the nitrogen gradient: pleural gas contains ~78% nitrogen, and inhaled oxygen displaces alveolar nitrogen, creating a steep partial pressure gradient that draws nitrogen out of the pleural space approximately fourfold faster than room air.

sources.label: TSANZ Pneumothorax position statement
Conservative versus Interventional Treatment for Spontaneous Pneumothorax (PSP trial), NEJM 2020

stem: A 64-year-old man with heart failure on furosemide presents with progressive dyspnoea and a left-sided pleural effusion. Diagnostic thoracentesis is performed. Pleural fluid analysis: protein 31 g/L, lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) 280 U/L; serum protein 64 g/L, serum LDH 220 U/L. Pleural fluid pH 7.42, glucose 5.6 mmol/L, no organisms on Gram stain, cytology pending. He received intravenous furosemide for 3 days prior to thoracentesis.

data_table.Pleural fluid: Protein 31 g/L, LDH 280 U/L, pH 7.42, glucose 5.6 mmol/L.
data_table.Recent treatment: IV furosemide 3 days prior to tap.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate interpretation?

options.text: Exudate by Light criteria; warrants exudate workup including CT chest
options.rationale: Light criteria classify exudate if fluid/serum protein >0.5, fluid/serum LDH >0.6, or fluid LDH >2/3 upper limit of normal. The LDH ratio (1.27) exceeds 0.6, classifying as exudate by Light criteria. However, Light criteria misclassify ~25% of heart failure effusions as exudate after diuresis (false exudate). The serum-pleural albumin gradient (or protein gradient) corrects for this.

options.text: Transudate consistent with heart failure given clinical context and diuretic effect
options.rationale: Light criteria label this exudate but the serum-to-pleural protein gradient is 64 - 31 = 33 g/L (>31 g/L excludes exudate). Diuresis concentrates pleural protein and LDH, falsely raising Light ratios. In a patient with clear heart failure on active diuresis, applying the protein gradient correctly reclassifies as transudate. No further exudate workup needed.

options.text: Parapneumonic effusion requiring intercostal catheter insertion
options.rationale: Parapneumonic effusion features include low pH (<7.2), low glucose (<2.2 mmol/L) and elevated LDH (>1000 U/L). None are present. Gram stain negative further argues against (eTG).

options.text: Malignant effusion; arrange pleurodesis as definitive management
options.rationale: Cytology is pending and there are no specific markers (bloody fluid, mass on imaging) directing to malignancy. Treatment ahead of cytology would be premature (eTG).

options.text: Tuberculous effusion; commence empirical antituberculous therapy
options.rationale: TB pleurisy classically shows lymphocyte-predominant exudate with high adenosine deaminase (>40 U/L). Glucose may be low. None are present. Empirical TB therapy is not warranted without microbiology or ADA (eTG).

explanation.summary: Light criteria classify pleural effusions as transudate vs exudate but misclassify 25% of diuresed heart failure effusions as exudate. The serum-pleural protein gradient (>31 g/L = transudate) and serum-pleural albumin gradient (>12 g/L = transudate) correct for diuretic effect.
key_points: Light criteria exudate: protein ratio >0.5, LDH ratio >0.6, or fluid LDH >2/3 ULN
Diuresis falsely raises Light ratios; use protein/albumin gradient to correct
Parapneumonic complicated: pH <7.2, glucose <2.2, LDH >1000 = drain
TB pleurisy: lymphocytic exudate, ADA >40 U/L, granulomas on biopsy
explanation.context: Pleural fluid forms from hydrostatic-oncotic imbalance (transudate) or capillary leak/lymphatic blockage (exudate). Light criteria use protein and LDH as surrogates for capillary permeability. Diuresis shrinks the watery component of a transudate, concentrating residual protein into the false-exudate range. The protein gradient method works because the absolute serum-pleural protein difference reflects the underlying mechanism more robustly than ratios that compress with diuresis.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory - Pleural disease
TSANZ Pleural Effusion guidelines

stem: A 58-year-old woman with community-acquired pneumonia not improving after 48 hours of intravenous ceftriaxone and azithromycin develops a moderate left-sided pleural effusion. Bedside ultrasound shows a complex septated collection. Thoracentesis: pleural fluid pH 7.05, glucose 1.6 mmol/L, LDH 1850 U/L, protein 48 g/L, white cell count 28 x10^9/L (neutrophil predominant), Gram stain shows gram-positive cocci in chains, no growth yet on culture.

data_table.Pleural fluid: pH 7.05, glucose 1.6 mmol/L, LDH 1850 U/L, protein 48 g/L, WCC 28 (neutrophilic).
data_table.Microbiology: Gram-positive cocci in chains on Gram stain; culture pending.
data_table.Imaging: Bedside US: moderate left effusion with multiple internal septations.
data_table.Treatment so far: 48 h IV ceftriaxone + azithromycin for CAP.
data_table.Clinical: Persistent fever, raised WCC, ongoing dyspnoea.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Intercostal catheter drainage and continue current antibiotics
options.rationale: Complicated parapneumonic effusion (pH <7.2, glucose <2.2 mmol/L, positive Gram stain) requires drainage with an intercostal catheter alongside antibiotics. Failure to drain risks progression to empyema, pleural fibrosis and chronic restriction. Septations on ultrasound predict drainage difficulty and may require intrapleural fibrinolytics.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - CAP and empyema

options.text: Continue antibiotics for 5 more days before considering drainage
options.rationale: Antibiotic-only management of complicated parapneumonic effusion has unacceptably high failure and empyema rates. Drainage criteria mandate intervention now (TSANZ).

options.text: Repeat diagnostic thoracentesis in 24 hours to reassess pH
options.rationale: Drainage criteria are already met; repeat thoracentesis delays definitive treatment and worsens outcomes. Diagnostic tap is for initial classification, not serial monitoring (TSANZ).

options.text: Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) decortication now
options.rationale: VATS is the next step if intercostal catheter drainage with intrapleural fibrinolytics fails. First-line drainage is small-bore intercostal catheter; surgery escalation follows failed medical drainage (TSANZ).

options.text: Broaden antibiotics to meropenem and vancomycin without drainage
options.rationale: Antibiotic broadening without drainage does not penetrate the loculated fluid effectively and ignores the source control imperative. Drainage is the priority; current antibiotic choice already covers Streptococcus species (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - CAP and empyema

explanation.summary: Complicated parapneumonic effusion or empyema requires source control through pleural drainage in addition to antibiotics. Drainage criteria are any of: pH <7.2, glucose <2.2 mmol/L, positive Gram stain or culture, frank pus, or loculated effusion. Failure of drainage at 72 hours warrants surgical referral.
key_points: Drainage criteria: pH <7.2, glucose <2.2, positive micro, or frank pus
Small-bore (12-14 Fr) ICC equivalent to large bore, less painful
Intrapleural tPA + DNase improves drainage in septated collections (MIST-2)
VATS decortication if ICC + fibrinolytics fail at 72 h
explanation.context: Parapneumonic effusion progresses through three stages: exudative (clear fluid, neutrophilic, drainage rarely needed); fibrinopurulent (loculations, low pH, low glucose, requires drainage); organising (thick peel, requires decortication). Bacterial metabolism within the pleural space consumes glucose and produces lactate (lowering pH); leucocyte release of LDH and granulocyte recruitment further amplify the inflammatory signature. Early recognition prevents progression to fibrotic chronic empyema.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Community-acquired pneumonia and empyema
TSANZ Pleural Effusion guidelines

stem: A 67-year-old woman with metastatic breast carcinoma presents with breathlessness from a large right-sided pleural effusion. Cytology of a previous thoracentesis confirmed malignant cells. She has had three therapeutic taps in the past 6 weeks, with rapid re-accumulation each time. She has a good performance status (ECOG 1) and life expectancy estimated at 9 to 12 months. Bedside ultrasound shows the lung re-expands fully after drainage. She prioritises symptom control and minimising hospital time.

data_table.Diagnosis: Metastatic breast cancer, cytology-positive malignant pleural effusion.
data_table.Recurrence pattern: 3 therapeutic thoracenteses in 6 weeks, rapid re-accumulation.
data_table.Lung re-expansion: Full re-expansion after drainage on US.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate definitive intervention?

options.text: Indwelling pleural catheter for ambulatory drainage at home
options.rationale: Indwelling pleural catheter (IPC) is one valid option, particularly when lung does not re-expand (trapped lung). With full lung re-expansion and good performance status, talc pleurodesis can offer a definitive solution with similar quality of life. Both are listed in TSANZ; choice is patient-centred.
source_refs: TSANZ Malignant Pleural Effusion position statement

options.text: Talc slurry pleurodesis via intercostal catheter
options.rationale: With full lung re-expansion on drainage, talc pleurodesis is highly effective (~70-80% success) and offers a single-procedure definitive option for patients with reasonable life expectancy. Talc induces pleural fibrosis through inflammatory adhesion. AMPLE trial (TSANZ-endorsed) compared IPC and pleurodesis with similar outcomes; selection is preference-based.
source_refs: TSANZ Malignant Pleural Effusion position statement

options.text: Continue intermittent therapeutic thoracentesis as required
options.rationale: Repeated thoracentesis is appropriate only when life expectancy is short (under 1 month) or both pleurodesis and IPC are unsuitable. With ~12 month prognosis and the burden of three taps in 6 weeks, a definitive intervention is warranted (TSANZ).
source_refs: TSANZ Malignant Pleural Effusion position statement

options.text: Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery and mechanical pleurodesis
options.rationale: VATS pleurodesis offers similar outcomes to bedside chemical pleurodesis but requires general anaesthesia and longer recovery. Reserved for situations where bedside approach has failed or diagnostic biopsy is also needed (TSANZ).
source_refs: TSANZ Malignant Pleural Effusion position statement

options.text: Systemic chemotherapy escalation without local pleural intervention
options.rationale: Systemic therapy may help over weeks but does not address acute symptoms from a tense effusion. Local control is the immediate need; systemic therapy continues in parallel (TSANZ).
source_refs: TSANZ Malignant Pleural Effusion position statement

explanation.summary: Recurrent malignant pleural effusion in a patient with months of life expectancy is managed with definitive control: talc pleurodesis if lung re-expands fully, or indwelling pleural catheter if trapped lung or patient preference favours ambulatory drainage. Both are first-line options under TSANZ.
key_points: Talc pleurodesis: 70-80% success when lung re-expands fully
IPC: equivalent symptom outcomes (AMPLE trial), suits trapped lung
Recurrent thoracentesis: only when prognosis <1 month or both refused
VATS pleurodesis: reserved for failure of bedside approach
explanation.context: Malignant effusions form when tumour seeds pleural surfaces, obstructing lymphatic drainage and producing exudate. Mean survival from diagnosis varies by primary (breast 7-15 months, lung 3-6, mesothelioma highly variable). The pleurodesis vs IPC choice hinges on lung re-expansion: trapped lung (from tumour rind or organised effusion) means apposition of pleural surfaces cannot occur, and talc cannot create the adhesion needed; IPC then provides ambulatory drainage without requiring fusion.

sources.label: TSANZ Malignant Pleural Effusion position statement
AMPLE trial - Thomas et al., JAMA 2017

stem: A 34-year-old man who arrived in Sydney from Vietnam 18 months ago presents with 6 weeks of low-grade fever, drenching night sweats, weight loss of 8 kg, and progressive right-sided pleuritic chest pain. Chest x-ray shows a moderate right pleural effusion without parenchymal consolidation. Thoracentesis: straw-coloured fluid, protein 52 g/L (serum 70), LDH 540 U/L (serum 240), pH 7.36, glucose 3.4 mmol/L, white cell count 4.8 x10^9/L (lymphocyte predominant 88%), adenosine deaminase (ADA) 78 U/L. Acid-fast bacilli smear is negative. Mantoux 18 mm.

data_table.Pleural fluid: Protein 52 g/L (S 70), LDH 540 (S 240), pH 7.36, glucose 3.4, WCC 4.8 (88% lymphs), ADA 78 U/L.
data_table.Imaging: CXR: moderate right effusion, no parenchymal consolidation.
data_table.Background: Migrated from Vietnam 18 months ago; constitutional symptoms 6 weeks.

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide and ethambutol after notification to NSW Health
options.rationale: Lymphocytic exudate with ADA above 40 U/L in a migrant from a high-TB-prevalence region has a positive predictive value of approximately 95% for tuberculous pleurisy. NSW Health TB Programme accepts empirical commencement of standard 4-drug therapy while culture is awaited, with mandatory notification. Pleural biopsy can confirm but is not required before treatment in classical presentations.

options.text: Defer treatment pending pleural biopsy and mycobacterial culture (6 weeks)
options.rationale: Pleural biopsy improves diagnostic yield to 80-90% (cell-block or VATS) but treatment should not wait 6 weeks for culture in a high-probability presentation with public health implications. Empirical treatment is the standard pathway (NSW Health).

options.text: Empirical antibiotics for community-acquired pneumonia and reassess in 1 week
options.rationale: The presentation (chronic constitutional symptoms, lymphocytic exudate, high ADA, high-prevalence background) is not consistent with bacterial pneumonia. Treating empirically for CAP delays appropriate therapy and risks TB progression and contact transmission (eTG).

options.text: Intercostal catheter drainage and pleurodesis to control reaccumulation
options.rationale: Tuberculous effusions usually resolve with anti-tuberculous therapy alone; pleurodesis is not indicated and may worsen residual fibrosis. Drainage is reserved for very large symptomatic collections after therapy commenced (NSW Health).

options.text: Quantiferon-TB Gold testing to confirm diagnosis before treatment
options.rationale: Quantiferon detects latent or active TB infection but cannot distinguish between them and adds nothing to a positive Mantoux in this clinical picture. Diagnostic stewardship favours moving to treatment now (NSW Health).

explanation.summary: Tuberculous pleurisy in Australia is most often seen in migrants from high-prevalence countries (Vietnam, China, India, Philippines, sub-Saharan Africa). The triad of lymphocyte-predominant exudate, elevated ADA (>40 U/L), and high pre-test probability supports empirical 4-drug therapy without waiting for culture, alongside mandatory NSW Health notification.
key_points: ADA >40 U/L in lymphocytic exudate: PPV ~95% in high-prevalence cohorts
AFB smear sensitivity in pleural fluid <10%; culture takes 4-6 weeks
Empirical 4-drug regimen: RIPE for 2 months then RI for 4 months
Notification mandatory under Public Health Act 2010; contact tracing
explanation.context: Tuberculous pleurisy arises from rupture of a subpleural caseous focus into the pleural space, eliciting a delayed hypersensitivity reaction that produces the lymphocytic exudate. ADA is elevated because T-lymphocytes activated against mycobacterial antigens secrete this enzyme in pleural fluid. The combination of high ADA, lymphocytic predominance and clinical context gives sufficient pre-test probability to treat empirically; awaiting culture risks both individual progression (miliary TB, chronic empyema) and community transmission.

sources.label: NSW Health Policy Directive PD2014_050 Tuberculosis Management
Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Tuberculosis

stem: Five patients present with confirmed pulmonary embolism complicated by haemodynamic instability requiring vasopressor support. The senior registrar is deciding which of the following features should be treated as an absolute (rather than relative) contraindication to systemic thrombolysis.

data_table.Patient A: Ischaemic stroke 4 weeks ago, fully recovered, on aspirin.
data_table.Patient C: Major orthopaedic surgery 2 weeks ago, wound healing well.
data_table.Patient D: Platelets 88 x10^9/L on a single course of chemotherapy.

lead_in: Which patient has an absolute contraindication to systemic thrombolysis?

options.text: Patient A - ischaemic stroke 4 weeks ago
options.rationale: Ischaemic stroke within 3 months IS an absolute contraindication per ESC and Heart Foundation, however 4 weeks falls within this window. This is correct as an absolute contraindication too, but the question asks for the single best example; patient B (active intracranial neoplasm) is the more unambiguous absolute contraindication that has no temporal qualifier and is universally listed.

options.text: Patient B - active intracranial neoplasm
options.rationale: Active intracranial neoplasm is an absolute contraindication to systemic thrombolysis across all major guidelines (Heart Foundation, ESC, BTS) because the bleeding risk into the CNS is unacceptable regardless of indication. The contraindication is permanent for the duration of disease.

options.text: Patient C - major orthopaedic surgery 2 weeks ago
options.rationale: Major surgery within 3 weeks is a relative contraindication; the bleeding risk is real but may be outweighed by life-threatening PE. Decision is individualised (ESC).

options.text: Patient D - platelets 88 x10^9/L
options.rationale: Thrombocytopenia is a relative contraindication; absolute thresholds vary but commonly <50 x10^9/L is considered significant. 88 is acceptable in shock-state PE (ESC).

options.text: Patient E - uncontrolled hypertension 178/108 mmHg
options.rationale: Severe uncontrolled hypertension (SBP >180 or DBP >110) is a relative contraindication. Treating the BP and proceeding with lysis is often appropriate in life-threatening PE (ESC).

explanation.summary: Absolute contraindications to systemic thrombolysis include any prior intracranial haemorrhage, ischaemic stroke within 3 months (not 4.5 hours for stroke lysis), known intracranial AVM or neoplasm, recent significant head trauma or surgery, suspected aortic dissection, and active bleeding diathesis. Relative contraindications include recent major non-CNS surgery, anticoagulation, controlled hypertension, advanced age, and pregnancy.
key_points: Absolute CNS contraindications: prior ICH, active intracranial neoplasm or AVM
Stroke within 3 months: absolute (different from 4.5 h ischaemic stroke window)
Recent major surgery: relative; weigh against PE mortality
Severe HTN: relative; control BP then proceed if PE is life-threatening
explanation.context: The absolute vs relative distinction matters because PE shock has 30-50% mortality untreated. Relative contraindications shift the risk-benefit equation but do not bar therapy when the alternative is death. CNS contraindications are absolute because the bleeding risk - though numerically small - is catastrophic if it occurs (massive intracerebral haemorrhage in pre-existing pathology). Active intracranial neoplasm is the cleanest example because the abnormal vasculature is a fixed bleeding substrate that no temporary measure can mitigate.

sources.label: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline (lytic contraindications)
ESC 2019 Acute Pulmonary Embolism Guidelines

stem: A 68-year-old woman with severe COPD (post-bronchodilator FEV1 28% predicted) on optimised inhaled triple therapy is reviewed at the respiratory clinic 6 weeks after an exacerbation. She has stopped smoking 8 months ago. Arterial blood gas on room air at rest: pH 7.41, pCO2 48 mmHg, pO2 54 mmHg, SpO2 88%, HCO3 30 mmol/L. Repeat ABG 3 weeks later under stable conditions: pO2 56 mmHg, SpO2 88%. She has secondary polycythaemia (haematocrit 0.58) and lower-limb peripheral oedema with raised JVP suggesting cor pulmonale.

data_table.ABG (stable, RA, on 2 occasions): pO2 54-56 mmHg, SpO2 88%, pCO2 48 mmHg, pH 7.41.
data_table.Right heart: Raised JVP, peripheral oedema, ECG p-pulmonale - cor pulmonale.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate decision regarding long-term oxygen therapy?

options.text: Commence home oxygen at 1-2 L/min for at least 15 hours per day
options.rationale: She meets Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand eligibility for long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT): stable COPD with pO2 <=55 mmHg, or 56-59 mmHg with polycythaemia, cor pulmonale or pulmonary hypertension. Two ABGs at least 3 weeks apart while clinically stable and off smoking confirm eligibility. LTOT reduces mortality when used for at least 15 hours per day (NOTT, MRC trials).
source_refs: TSANZ Adult Domiciliary Oxygen Therapy guidelines

options.text: Continue current therapy and reassess for LTOT in 6 months
options.rationale: She already meets criteria after stable repeat assessment. Delaying LTOT denies proven mortality benefit (TSANZ).
source_refs: TSANZ Adult Domiciliary Oxygen Therapy guidelines

options.text: Commence nocturnal-only home oxygen as initial trial
options.rationale: Nocturnal-only oxygen does not improve mortality in COPD with chronic daytime hypoxaemia. LTOT requires >=15 hours/day (and ideally >18 hours/day) for survival benefit (TSANZ).
source_refs: TSANZ Adult Domiciliary Oxygen Therapy guidelines

options.text: Defer until smoking cessation is verified for 12 months
options.rationale: TSANZ recommends smoking cessation before LTOT primarily for fire safety, but a documented 8-month period of cessation with biochemical confirmation can satisfy this criterion. 12 months is not a uniform requirement (TSANZ).
source_refs: TSANZ Adult Domiciliary Oxygen Therapy guidelines

options.text: Therapeutic venesection for polycythaemia before considering oxygen
options.rationale: Polycythaemia in chronic hypoxia is the body's adaptive response; reversing it without correcting hypoxaemia reduces oxygen-carrying capacity. Address the underlying hypoxia with LTOT and the polycythaemia will resolve (TSANZ).
source_refs: TSANZ Adult Domiciliary Oxygen Therapy guidelines

explanation.summary: Australian LTOT criteria require stable resting hypoxaemia confirmed on two ABGs at least 3 weeks apart while not actively exacerbating: pO2 <=55 mmHg outright, OR pO2 56-59 mmHg with cor pulmonale, polycythaemia or pulmonary hypertension. Use >=15 h/day reduces mortality; >18 h/day is preferred.
key_points: Two ABGs >=3 weeks apart while stable, post-exacerbation recovery, on optimised therapy
pO2 <=55 mmHg = standalone criterion; 56-59 needs polycythaemia/cor pulmonale/PHT
Smoking cessation required for safety (fire risk with oxygen)
>=15 h/day for survival benefit; nocturnal-only insufficient
explanation.context: The NOTT (US) and MRC (UK) trials in the early 1980s established that continuous oxygen for chronically hypoxic COPD doubled 5-year survival versus no oxygen, and that nearly-continuous (>18 h) outperformed nocturnal-only. Mechanism is reversal of hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction, lowering pulmonary artery pressure, unloading the right ventricle and reducing the polycythaemic drive. Crucially, LTOT does not improve survival in patients with milder hypoxaemia, exercise-only desaturation, or nocturnal-only desaturation.

sources.label: TSANZ Adult Domiciliary Oxygen Therapy guidelines
Lung Foundation Australia COPD-X Concise Guide

subtopic_detail: Side-effect to drug mapping - inhaled therapy
stem: A 45-year-old man with asthma develops oral candidiasis, dysphonia and easy skin bruising 6 months after starting a new asthma controller. He uses the device twice daily with adequate technique. The respiratory pharmacist asks the registrar to identify which medication class is most likely responsible.

data_table.Adverse effects: Oral candidiasis (white plaques on tongue/buccal mucosa), dysphonia (hoarse voice), easy skin bruising on forearms.
data_table.Duration of use: 6 months on new controller, twice daily, good inhaler technique.
data_table.Other meds: Salbutamol PRN, no oral corticosteroid courses in past 12 months.
data_table.Examination: Multiple ecchymoses forearms, oral thrush confirmed on swab.

lead_in: Which medication class is most likely responsible?

options.text: Inhaled corticosteroid
options.rationale: Oral candidiasis, dysphonia (from laryngeal muscle myopathy or local steroid deposition) and dermal thinning with bruising are signature inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) effects. Local effects (candidiasis, dysphonia) are reduced by spacer use and post-dose mouth rinsing; systemic effects (bruising, adrenal suppression, osteoporosis with high doses) reflect pulmonary absorption.

options.text: Long-acting beta agonist (LABA)
options.rationale: LABA effects are predominantly cardiovascular (palpitations, tachycardia, fine tremor, occasional hypokalaemia). Candidiasis, dysphonia and bruising are not LABA effects (eTG).

options.text: Long-acting muscarinic antagonist (LAMA)
options.rationale: LAMA effects reflect antimuscarinic activity: dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation, precipitation of glaucoma. Candidiasis, dysphonia and bruising are not LAMA effects (eTG).

options.text: Leukotriene receptor antagonist (montelukast)
options.rationale: Montelukast effects include neuropsychiatric (FDA boxed warning for depression and suicidality), sleep disturbance, and rarely Churg-Strauss syndrome. Candidiasis, dysphonia and bruising are not montelukast effects (eTG).

options.text: Theophylline
options.rationale: Theophylline causes nausea, headache, tremor, tachyarrhythmia and seizures at toxic levels. It is rarely used in modern Australian practice. Candidiasis, dysphonia and bruising are not theophylline effects (eTG).

explanation.summary: The triad of oral candidiasis, dysphonia and dermal thinning is highly specific for inhaled corticosteroid effect. Local effects reflect deposition in oropharynx and larynx; systemic effects reflect pulmonary absorption (10-50% of dose). Spacer use, mouth rinsing and lowest effective dose minimise all categories.
key_points: ICS local effects: candidiasis, dysphonia, sore throat (spacer + rinse reduce)
ICS systemic effects: bruising, adrenal suppression, osteoporosis, cataract, growth velocity reduction in children
LABA: tremor, tachycardia, hypokalaemia
LAMA: dry mouth, urinary retention, glaucoma exacerbation
explanation.context: Despite inhaled delivery, ICS reach systemic circulation via pulmonary absorption (which mostly bypasses first-pass metabolism) and via swallowed fraction. Higher-potency molecules (fluticasone propionate) have more systemic effect than newer molecules (ciclesonide, mometasone furoate) that undergo more first-pass deactivation. Dermal thinning with bruising on sun-exposed areas (forearms, dorsum of hands) is one of the earliest visible signs of systemic steroid exposure and should prompt dose review, technique check and consideration of step-down.

sources.label: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)
Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory - Asthma

subtopic_detail: ARDS - ventilator strategy and prone positioning
stem: A 56-year-old man admitted with severe community-acquired pneumonia has been intubated and ventilated for 36 hours in the intensive care unit. Despite optimised therapy he meets the Berlin definition of acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS). Current ventilator settings: volume control, tidal volume 7 mL/kg predicted body weight, respiratory rate 20, FiO2 0.8, positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) 12. PaO2/FiO2 ratio 92 mmHg. He has not been pharmacologically paralysed. Plateau pressure 32 cmH2O. No pneumothorax. Bilateral patchy infiltrates on chest x-ray.

data_table.Vent settings: VC, TV 7 mL/kg PBW, RR 20, FiO2 0.8, PEEP 12, P-plateau 32 cmH2O.
data_table.Background: Severe CAP, 36 h intubated, Berlin criteria met.

lead_in: Which intervention is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Prone position the patient for at least 16 hours per day
options.rationale: Severe ARDS (PaO2/FiO2 <150) refractory to lung-protective ventilation has a mortality benefit from prone positioning for >=16 hours per day (PROSEVA trial). Prone positioning recruits dorsal atelectasis (the dorsal lung is the dependent region when supine), homogenises ventilation-perfusion matching, and reduces ventilator-induced lung injury.
source_refs: ANZICS Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome position statement

options.text: Increase tidal volume to 10 mL/kg to improve minute ventilation
options.rationale: Lung-protective ventilation in ARDS uses tidal volume of 4-8 mL/kg predicted body weight and plateau pressure <=30 cmH2O. Increasing tidal volume worsens ventilator-induced lung injury through volutrauma and atelectrauma (ARDSNet ARMA trial; ANZICS).
source_refs: ANZICS Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome position statement

options.text: Switch to high-frequency oscillatory ventilation
options.rationale: OSCAR and OSCILLATE trials showed no benefit or harm from HFOV in ARDS. It is no longer recommended (ANZICS).
source_refs: ANZICS Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome position statement

options.text: Reduce PEEP to 5 cmH2O to lower plateau pressure
options.rationale: Reducing PEEP would worsen alveolar derecruitment and oxygenation. Plateau pressure 32 is at the upper acceptable limit; the answer is to reduce driving pressure by lowering tidal volume slightly or improving compliance through proning, not by withdrawing PEEP (ANZICS).
source_refs: ANZICS Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome position statement

options.text: Commence inhaled nitric oxide at 20 parts per million
options.rationale: Inhaled nitric oxide improves oxygenation transiently in ARDS but has no mortality benefit and increases renal injury risk. Reserved as a temporising bridge in life-threatening hypoxaemia awaiting other rescue therapies (ANZICS).
source_refs: ANZICS Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome position statement

explanation.summary: ARDS management is built on lung-protective ventilation (TV 4-8 mL/kg PBW, P-plateau <=30, driving pressure <=15). Severe ARDS (PaO2/FiO2 <150) escalates to prone positioning >=16 h/day (PROSEVA), neuromuscular blockade (ACURASYS - debated), and ECMO referral (EOLIA). HFOV and nitric oxide are not first-line.
key_points: Lung-protective: TV 4-8 mL/kg PBW, P-plateau <=30, driving pressure <=15
PROSEVA: prone >=16 h/day reduces mortality in severe ARDS (P/F <150)
ECMO referral: PaO2/FiO2 <80 despite optimisation, or P-plateau >30 to achieve TV 6
PEEP titration: use higher PEEP for moderate-severe ARDS (P/F <200)
explanation.context: ARDS is characterised by diffuse alveolar damage with non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, reduced compliance and severe hypoxaemia. Lung-protective ventilation reduces mortality (ARMA trial 1999) because high tidal volumes cause cyclical alveolar overdistension (volutrauma) and shear stress at the interface of aerated and collapsed lung (atelectrauma). Proning works through gravitational redistribution: supine, the dorsal lung is dependent, atelectatic and poorly aerated while still well-perfused (shunt). Proning reverses dependency, recruits dorsal alveoli, homogenises ventilation-perfusion matching, and reduces shunt fraction. PROSEVA demonstrated a 16% absolute mortality reduction in severe ARDS with this single intervention.

sources.label: ANZICS Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome position statement
PROSEVA trial - Guerin et al., NEJM 2013

stem: A 38-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with 12 hours of pleuritic right-sided chest pain and mild dyspnoea. She is on the combined oral contraceptive pill, has no prior venous thromboembolism, and recently completed a 5-hour flight from Perth 3 days ago. Examination is unremarkable. Heart rate 92, blood pressure 124/76, SpO2 97% room air, respiratory rate 18, afebrile. Two-level Wells score for PE: 3 (clinical signs of DVT 0, alternative diagnosis less likely 3, HR >100 0, immobilisation/surgery 0, prior DVT/PE 0, haemoptysis 0, malignancy 0).

data_table.Examination: Unremarkable; no DVT signs, no haemoptysis.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Age-adjusted D-dimer measurement
options.rationale: Two-level Wells PE-unlikely (<=4) is followed by D-dimer testing. A negative age-adjusted D-dimer (age x 10 mcg/L for over-50s; conventional <500 for under-50s) rules out PE without imaging. CTPA is reserved for positive D-dimer or PE-likely Wells (>4).

options.text: Immediate computed tomography pulmonary angiogram
options.rationale: CTPA in PE-unlikely patients without D-dimer leads to unnecessary contrast exposure, radiation and over-diagnosis of subsegmental PE. D-dimer reduces imaging by 30% in this group (eTG).

options.text: Ventilation-perfusion (V/Q) scan
options.rationale: V/Q scan is appropriate when CTPA is contraindicated (severe contrast allergy, renal impairment, pregnancy) and the chest x-ray is clear. It is not the next step in a Wells-unlikely workup before D-dimer (TSANZ).

options.text: Bilateral lower limb compression ultrasound
options.rationale: Leg ultrasound looks for deep vein thrombosis but does not exclude PE if negative. It can be a useful adjunct when imaging exposure must be minimised (pregnancy) but is not first-line in PE-unlikely workup (TSANZ).

options.text: Empirical therapeutic anticoagulation pending further workup
options.rationale: Empirical anticoagulation in PE-unlikely patients without diagnostic confirmation exposes to bleeding risk without evidence-based indication. It is reserved for PE-likely patients with delays to imaging exceeding 4 hours (eTG).

explanation.summary: PE workup uses pretest probability (Wells or revised Geneva) to direct testing. PE-unlikely (Wells <=4): D-dimer; if negative, PE excluded. PE-likely (Wells >4): CTPA directly. Age-adjusted D-dimer cut-off (age x 10 mcg/L for over-50s) reduces false positives without missing cases.
key_points: Wells <=4 = unlikely; D-dimer rules out if negative
Wells >4 = likely; proceed directly to CTPA
Age-adjusted D-dimer: age x 10 mcg/L for those over 50
Pregnancy/contrast allergy: V/Q scan or leg US instead of CTPA
explanation.context: D-dimer is a fibrin degradation product elevated whenever cross-linked fibrin is being lysed. It has high sensitivity for VTE (>95%) but poor specificity (rises with age, infection, malignancy, pregnancy, recent surgery). The age-adjusted threshold (instead of fixed 500 mcg/L) corrects for the rise in baseline D-dimer with age, recovering specificity in older patients without sacrificing sensitivity. The PE-unlikely + negative D-dimer pathway has a 3-month VTE rate <1%, comparable to a negative CTPA, while avoiding imaging in 30% of patients.
